#define NECK_SERVO_MAX 600  // +90 degrees
#define NECK_SERVO_MID 375  // 0 degrees (Center)

#endif

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the fail-safe mechanisms and watchdog timers used to detect and recover from system faults.

docs.title: Hardware Watchdog (WDT)
docs.text: All ESP32 nodes enable the internal hardware watchdog timer. * Task Watchdog Timer (TWDT): Core tasks must "reset" or "feed" the watchdog at the end of each loop cycle. If a task crashes or becomes stuck in a blocking loop for more than 1.0 second, the TWDT triggers a system reset. * Interrupt Watchdog Timer (IWDT): Monitors high-priority hardware interrupts.

docs.title: Software Exception Handling
docs.text:
• Out of Memory (OOM): Memory allocation is verified. If malloc() fails to allocate memory, the system logs an allocation error, clears temporary caches, and restarts if necessary.
  • I2C Bus Recovery: If an I2C device (like the PCA9685 PWM driver) locks the clock line (SCL) or data line (SDA) to a low state, the node runs a recovery routine. This toggles the SCL line 9 times to clear the bus and re-initializes the wire library.

docs.title: Safe State Defaults
docs.text:
If a critical error is detected, the robot enters a safe state: 

  [ Fault Detected ] ──> [ Drive PWM set to 0% ] ──> [ Enable lines set to LOW ]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document outlines the firmware design pattern of the PRAYAS V1 microcontrollers. It provides rules for writing non-blocking, multi-core, and event-driven firmware.

docs.title: Architecture & Principles
docs.text:
PRAYAS V1 microcontrollers run C++ firmware written within the ESP-IDF framework or Arduino Core for ESP32. The architecture is modular:

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   Application Layer                     │
  │     (Task Handlers, State Controller, Command Parsers)   │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                    Middleware Layer                     │
  │     (ESP-NOW Router, MQTT Manager, Trajectory Engine)   │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                 Hardware Abstraction                    │
  │         (Drivers: BTS7960, PCA9685, LSM9DS1 IMU)        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  • Real-time Responsiveness: Time-critical calculations (such as PID loop updates, stepper/motor PWM output modifications, and incoming packet routing) run as high-priority FreeRTOS tasks.
  • Asynchronous Queues: Comm tasks write incoming actions to thread-safe FreeRTOS queues. Target loops fetch commands from these queues, preventing timing blocks.
  • Non-Volatile Storage (NVS): Calibration matrices, servo offsets, and Wi-Fi credentials are saved to the ESP32's NVS partition.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the layout of the code repository, ensuring code consistency across developer teams.

docs.title: Folder Tree
docs.text:
PRAYAS/
├── firmware/
│   ├── master/                 # Master Coordinator ESP32 codebase
│   │   ├── src/                # Source files (.cpp)
│   │   ├── include/            # Header files (.h)
│   │   └── platformio.ini      # Build configuration file
│   ├── motor/                  # Motor Controller ESP32 codebase
│   ├── servo/                  # Servo Controller ESP32 codebase
│   ├── ai_s3/                  # AI S3 Voice assistant codebase
│   ├── camera_cam/             # ESP32-CAM video stream code
│   ├── sensor_nano/            # Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense code
│   └── shared/                 # Common headers (ESP-NOW structures, protocols)
├── hardware/
│   ├── cad/                    # 3D models and assembly files
│   └── schematics/             # Circuit diagrams and PCB layouts
├── web_dashboard/              # React/HTML Frontend dashboard source code
├── scripts/                    # Calibration and testing scripts
└── docs/                       # MkDocs documentation project

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document defines the task schedule, priority queue, stack allocation, and core pinning configuration for all ESP32 nodes running FreeRTOS.

docs.title: Task Allocation
docs.text: ESP32 microcontrollers feature a dual-core Xtensa processor (Core 0 and Core 1). Tasks are pinned to specific cores to isolate processing:

docs.title: Master Node Task Allocations
docs.text: Task Name Priority Core Stack Size (Bytes) Primary Function vMQTTPoolTask 3 0 4096 Manages connection to MQTT broker, processes incoming control messages. vESPNOWRouter 5 1 2048 Transmits sensor updates and motor speed packets to sub-nodes. vHeartbeatTask 6 1 1024 Broadcasts heartbeat packets to active sub-nodes every 200 ms. vDiagnostics 1 0 2048 Reports free heap space and task stack high-water marks.

docs.title: Motor Node Task Allocations
docs.text: Task Name Priority Core Stack Size (Bytes) Primary Function vESPNOWRecv 5 0 2048 Receives and parses target velocities from the Master Node. vMotorPID 6 1 4096 Computes wheel speeds (20 kHz PWM rate) for the H-bridge. vProximityTask 4 0 1024 Reads 3 digital outputs of E18-D80NK IR proximity sensors for obstacle checking.

docs.title: Code Pattern: Creating Non-Blocking Tasks
docs.text:
void vMotorPIDTask(void *pvParameters) {
    TickType_t xLastWakeTime = xTaskGetTickCount();
    const TickType_t xFrequency = pdMS_TO_TICKS(10); // 100 Hz Loop

    for (;;) {
        // 1. Read sensors (encoders, IMU)
        // 2. Run PID calculation
        // 3. Output PWM duty cycles to BTS7960

        // Block task until next execution interval
        vTaskDelayUntil(&xLastWakeTime, xFrequency);
    }
}

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document lists the third-party libraries used in the PRAYAS V1 firmware, specifying their versions and purposes.

docs.title: ESP32 Core Library Set
docs.text:
• WiFi (Built-in): Manages network connections.
  • PubSubClient (by Nick O'Leary) [v2.8.0]: A lightweight MQTT client used for sending telemetry and receiving commands.
  • Adafruit PWM Servo Driver Library [v3.0.1]: Controls the PCA9685 over I2C.
  • ArduinoJson (by Benoit Blanchon) [v7.0.4]: Used for serializing and parsing JSON messages.
  • ES8311 (Custom audio driver): Controls the I2S register map for audio codecs.
  • ESPAsyncWebServer (by Me-No-Dev) [v3.0.6]: An asynchronous web server used for serving the live video stream from the ESP32-CAM.
  • ArduinoBLE (by Arduino) [v1.3.6]: Handles BLE communication on the Nano 33 BLE Sense.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the logging framework, output levels, and diagnostic message formats for the PRAYAS V1 robot.

docs.title: Log Severity Levels
docs.text:
Log messages are classified using standard severity levels to filter debug information:

  1. VERBOSE (V): Detailed debugging information (e.g., individual sensor reads).
  2. DEBUG (D): Node state transitions and telemetry updates.
  3. INFO (I): System events (e.g., Wi-Fi connected, client connected).
  4. WARN (W): Non-critical anomalies (e.g., packet drop, high sensor readings).
  5. ERROR (E): Event failures that need immediate attention (e.g., I2C bus lockup).

docs.title: Log Message Format
docs.text: Log messages are sent over the serial interface (115200 baud) and compiled into a single diagnostic MQTT stream: [TIMESTAMP_MS] [NODE_NAME] [SEVERITY] [MESSAGE]

docs.title: Log Output Example
docs.text:
00010452 [MASTER] [I] WiFi connected to SSID: PRAYAS_ROBOT_NET
00010488 [MASTER] [I] IP address: 192.168.1.105
00010502 [MASTER] [D] ESP-NOW Peer Registered: Servo Node (24:0A:C4:81:32:0A)
00010950 [SERVO]  [W] Servo 3 offset out of bounds. Adjusting to limit.
00011500 [MOTOR]  [E] Center IR proximity sensor triggered obstacle halt.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the Over-the-Air (OTA) update mechanism, which allows developers to update firmware on the nodes wirelessly.

docs.title: OTA Architecture
docs.text:
PRAYAS V1 supports OTA updates via two modes:

  [ Developer PC ] ───(HTTP POST)───> [ Master Node ]
                                            │
                                    (Checks Partition)
                                            │
                                     [ Writes to OTA ]
  1. Direct Web OTA: The Master Node hosts a simple web page on port 80 (accessible when in Local Access Point Mode) containing a file upload form.
  2. ArduinoOTA Utility: Uses the standard multicast DNS (mDNS) protocol, allowing developers to upload compiled binaries directly from the IDE (PlatformIO or Arduino) over the local Wi-Fi network.

docs.title: Partition Table Configuration
docs.text:
To support OTA updates, the ESP32's flash memory must be partitioned to hold two application binaries (ota_0 and ota_1) along with the NVS partition:

+--------------------------------------------------------+
|                      ESP32 Flash                       |
+------------+------------+---------------+--------------+
| Bootloader | Partition  |  Application  | Application  |
|   (24KB)   | Table (4KB)|  ota_0 (1.5MB)| ota_1 (1.5MB)|
+------------+------------+---------------+--------------+
If an OTA update fails, the bootloader automatically reverts to the previously working application partition.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the conversation pipeline, explaining how natural language is converted into actions and audio feedback.

docs.title: Conversational Pipeline Flow
docs.text:
The conversation system processes audio in five distinct stages:

sequenceDiagram
    actor User
    participant AI as AI Node (ESP32-S3)
    participant VPS as VPS Server
    participant LLM as LLM Engine
    participant Master as Master Node

    User->>AI: Speaks: "Hey Prayas, wave your hand."
    AI->>AI: Wake word detected offline
    AI->>VPS: Starts audio stream (WebSockets)
    AI->>User: Plays tone (Listen Active)
    User->>AI: Speaks command
    AI->>VPS: Streams raw voice
    VPS->>LLM: Speech-to-Text (STT) + Prompt
    LLM->>LLM: Process intent
    LLM->>Master: Send action payload (Gesture: wave)
    LLM->>VPS: Generate response text
    VPS->>AI: Streams audio response (TTS)
    AI->>User: Play speech audio

docs.title: Performance Targets
docs.text:
• Wake Word Detection: Done locally on the ESP32-S3 in under 150 ms.
  • Speech-to-Text (STT) & LLM Response: Under 800 ms.
  • Text-to-Speech (TTS) Generation: Streaming playback begins within 400 ms of receiving text.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document describes the intent parsing system, which identifies user commands from text and routes them to the appropriate robot subsystems.

docs.title: Intent Parsing Logic
docs.text:
Once the user's speech is converted to text, it is processed to determine the intent and extract parameters:

  User says: "Move forward half a meter."
      │
      ▼ (Text parsed by LLM/NLP)
  Intent: MOVE_DISTANCE
  Parameters: { direction: "forward", distance: 0.5 }
      │
      ▼ (Command sent to Master)
  Master issues ESP-NOW packet to Motor Node

docs.title: Predefined Intents Table
docs.text: Intent Name Example Utterance Target Subsystem Action ROBOT_MOVE "Go forward a bit." Motor Node Sets target linear velocity. ROBOT_GESTURE "Wave your left hand." Servo Node Triggers arm gesture. SYSTEM_STATUS "How is your battery?" Power Node Speaks current battery charge level. ROBOT_STOP "Stop moving now!" Motor Node Instantly stops the wheels.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the integration of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) on the AI Node, which allows LLMs to query sensors and control the robot's physical systems.

docs.title: MCP Architecture
docs.text:
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) defines a client-server architecture where the LLM acts as the client and the PRAYAS Master Node runs an MCP server. This setup exposes the robot's hardware interfaces as tools that the model can call.

       +-------------------+
       |    Cloud LLM      |
       +---------┬---------+
                 │
                 │ (MCP JSON-RPC over WebSockets)
                 ▼
       +-------------------+
       | PRAYAS MCP Server | (Runs on VPS / Master Node)
       +---------┬---------+
                 │
                 ├─ Tool Call: drive_robot(0.5, 0.0) ──> Motor Node
                 └─ Tool Call: get_battery_voltage()  ──> Power Node

docs.title: 1. drive_robot(linear_x, angular_z)
docs.text:
• Description: Drives the robot's wheeled base at a target speed.
  • Parameters:
    • linear_x (float): Forward velocity (m/s), range -0.8 to 0.8.
    • angular_z (float): Rotation speed (rad/s), range -1.5 to 1.5.

docs.title: 2. trigger_gesture(gesture_name)
docs.text:
• Description: Triggers a predefined gesture on the arm or head servos.
  • Parameters:
    • gesture_name (string): Options include "wave", "point", "nod", "shake_head".

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document describes the Action Execution Engine, which coordinates physical movements, speech responses, and expressions.

docs.title: Coordinated Actions
docs.text:
The robot can coordinate movements, speech, and expressions to make interactions feel more natural.

sequenceDiagram
    participant Master as Master Node
    participant Audio as AI Node (Speaker)
    participant Servo as Servo Node (Joints)
    participant Screen as Display Node

    Master->>Screen: Display "Talking" Expression
    Master->>Audio: Play Audio: "Sure, let me point that out."
    Note over Audio, Servo: Action synchronization offset: 100ms
    Master->>Servo: Rotate Head to point direction
    Master->>Servo: Raise Left Arm to point
    Audio->>Master: Playback Finished
    Master->>Servo: Return Arm to home position
    Master->>Screen: Display "Idle" Expression

docs.title: Failure Recovery
docs.text: If an arm servo becomes overloaded or stuck during an action, it sends a telemetry alert. The Master Node then halts the current action, returns the arm to a safe position, and uses the text-to-speech system to notify the user.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document describes the Speech-to-Text configuration and performance characteristics of PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: STT Pipeline
docs.text: Because microcontrollers lack the processing power to run large-scale speech recognition locally, the AI Node streams voice data to a cloud service. * Input Format: I2S 16 kHz, 16-bit Mono PCM. * Processing: Streams audio packets over WebSockets to the cloud server, which uses OpenAI Whisper or Google Speech-to-Text to perform real-time translation. * VAD (Voice Activity Detection): The AI Node uses a local VAD algorithm to determine when the user starts and stops speaking. This reduces bandwidth consumption by only sending audio data when active speech is detected.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the Text-to-Speech engine configuration, audio format, and streaming playback mechanism for PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: TTS Configuration
docs.text:
• Engine: Edge-TTS or OpenAI TTS.
  • Audio Format: Streams MP3 or WAV (24 kHz sample rate, 16-bit Mono).
  • Streaming Mode: The AI Node uses a ring-buffer to play audio data as it arrives, avoiding the need to download the entire audio file before starting playback.

docs.title: Connection Diagram
docs.text: [ Cloud TTS Server ] ──(Audio Stream)──> [ ESP32-S3 AI Node ] ──(I2S)──> [ ES8311 DAC ] ──> [ Amp & Speaker ]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the vision tracking and processing capabilities of PRAYAS V1, explaining the division of labor between the Seeed Studio XIAO ESP32-S3 Sense integrated camera (AI Node) and the ESP32-CAM (Camera Node).

docs.title: Vision Architecture & Tasks Allocation
docs.text:
To maximize processing efficiency and maintain real-time performance, PRAYAS V1 isolates video streaming from AI cognitive vision tasks:

  1. AI Node Vision (Seeed Studio XIAO ESP32-S3 Sense):

    • Primary Tasks: Object recognition, face tracking, color tracking, and visual scene Q&A.
    • Implementation: Raw frames captured by the S3 on-board OV2640 camera are evaluated locally using lightweight CNNs or sent to cloud model endpoints (such as GPT-4o or Claude via the voice pipeline) for cognitive visual analysis.
    • Tracking Loop: 
       [ XIAO S3 Camera ] ──> [ Frame Capture ] ──> [ Local CNN Object Detection ]
                                                       │
                                            Is Target Identified?
                                             ├── Yes ──> [ Calculate Joint Target Offset ] ──> [ Servo Node ]
                                             └── No  ──> [ Scan/Idle Pattern ]

  2. Camera Node Vision (ESP32-CAM):

    • Primary Tasks: Dedicated live video feed stream.
    • Implementation: Runs a WebSocket/HTTP JPEG streaming server directly streaming video frames to the Web Dashboard at 12–15 fps. It does not perform local computer vision logic to avoid latency and thermal throttling.

docs.title: Performance and Limitations
docs.text:
• XIAO ESP32-S3 Sense: By separating tasks, the AI Node's processor can run local neural net tasks (e.g., face matching) at 10–12 fps at QVGA resolution.
  • ESP32-CAM: The standalone module serves smooth video without dropping frames when the robot is moving.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document describes the voice assistant configuration of PRAYAS V1, outlining the audio pipeline and natural language interaction flow.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The voice assistant runs on the AI Node (ESP32-S3) using the Xiaozhi Voice Framework. It captures audio using a microphone array, performs wake word detection, sends the voice stream to a local or cloud LLM, and plays back the speech response through a DAC and speaker.

graph LR
    User([User]) -->|Voice Input| Mic[INMP441 Mic]
    Mic -->|I2S Stream| AI[ESP32-S3 AI Node]
    AI -->|VAD / Wake word| AI
    AI -->|WebSocket Stream| Cloud[Xiaozhi Cloud VPS]
    Cloud -->|LLM / TTS Engine| Cloud
    Cloud -->|Audio Streams| AI
    AI -->|I2S Audio| DAC[ES8311 DAC]
    DAC -->|Analog Audio| Speaker[Speaker]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document describes the integration of the Xiaozhi open-source voice framework on the ESP32-S3 AI Node.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Xiaozhi framework is an open-source voice assistant framework designed for microcontrollers. It provides: * Local Wake Word Engine (using Espressif's ESP-SR library). * Low-latency WebSocket streaming. * Protocols for TTS playback streaming and intent-based JSON command delivery.

docs.title: Node Connection Settings
docs.text:
• Server Host: Deployed on a Virtual Private Server (VPS) at voice.prayas-robotics.org.
  • Protocol: WebSocket over secure TLS (port 443).
  • Payload Format: Protobuf or binary JSON for optimized transmission speed.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the autonomous locomotion, path planning, and obstacle avoidance systems of PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: Obstacle Avoidance Flowchart
docs.text:
Locomotion tasks default to a safety-monitored behavior using the three base-mounted E18-D80NK adjustable infrared proximity sensors (Left, Center, Right):

flowchart TD
    Start([Start Pathing]) --> ReadSensors[Read 3x E18-D80NK IR Sensors]
    ReadSensors --> CheckIR{Is Left, Center, or Right LOW?}

    CheckIR -- Yes --> StopBase[Halt Motors]
    StopBase --> BackUp[Reverse 10cm]
    BackUp --> SpinBase[Rotate 45 degrees away from trigger]
    SpinBase --> ReadSensors

    CheckIR -- No --> DriveForward[Drive toward target coordinates]
    DriveForward --> ReadSensors

docs.title: Odometry & Path Tracking
docs.text:
• Open-Loop Target Pathing: In V1, the robot estimates its position using wheel rotation duration and velocity profiles.
  • Odometry Limitations: Without wheel encoders, slippage on smooth floors can cause positioning errors (drift) of up to 15% over a 5-meter drive.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document explains the control priority hierarchy of PRAYAS V1, detailing how overlapping commands from different control sources (Gamepad, Voice, Web, Autonomous) are resolved.

docs.title: Priority Arbitration Model
docs.text:
The Master Node processes commands according to a strict priority hierarchy:

  Priority 1: Emergency Stop (E-Stop) Payload  (Overrides all inputs)
  Priority 2: Gamepad Manual Override Commands
  Priority 3: Web Dashboard Manual Overrides
  Priority 4: AI Voice Intent Commands
  Priority 5: Autonomous Locomotion / Obstacle Avoidance Pathing

docs.title: Arbitration Logic
docs.text:
• Active Control Window: When a manual control input (Gamepad or Web Panel) is received, the Master Node locks the system for 3.0 seconds, ignoring lower-priority autonomous commands.
  • Watchdog Release: If no manual input is received within the 3.0-second window, control reverts to the autonomous navigation task.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the configuration, button mappings, and signal routing for manual gamepad control of PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: Gamepad Mapping Specification
docs.text: The robot supports standard Bluetooth gamepads (e.g., PS4, Xbox, or generic BLE controllers) paired either directly with the Master Node (BLE) or routed via the Web Dashboard (HTML5 Gamepad API).

docs.title: Controller Button Layout
docs.text:
[L2] analog brake                [R2] analog throttle
         [L1] action trigger              [R1] action trigger

             (up)                             [Y] Point
        (left)  (right)                  [X] Wave    [B] Bow
            (down)                            [A] Neutral

            [L-Stick]                        [R-Stick]
        Base Locomotion                  Head Pitch/Yaw
        (Linear & Angular)               (Camera Pan)
Input Element Controller Output Master Command Mapping Left Stick Y-Axis \(-1.0\) to \(+1.0\) Linear base speed (\(v_x\)), forward/backward Left Stick X-Axis \(-1.0\) to \(+1.0\) Angular base speed (\(\omega_z\)), yaw rotation Right Stick Y-Axis \(-1.0\) to \(+1.0\) Neck Pitch angle increment Right Stick X-Axis \(-1.0\) to \(+1.0\) Neck Yaw angle increment Button X Trigger (Digital) Waves left hand (TRIGGER_GESTURE_1) Button Y Trigger (Digital) Points forward with right arm (TRIGGER_GESTURE_2) Button B Trigger (Digital) Lowers head and arms in a bow posture Button A Trigger (Digital) Commands all joints to return to home positions

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the motor control loops, PWM generation, acceleration ramps, and turning calculations for PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: Drive Dynamics & Kinematics
docs.text:
PRAYAS V1 uses a differential drive configuration. Given the robot's track width (\(L = 320 ext{ mm}\)) and wheel radius (\(R = 50 ext{ mm}\)), the target rotational speeds for the left (\(v_L\)) and right (\(v_R\)) wheels are calculated from target linear (\(v\)) and angular (\(\omega\)) velocities:

\[v_L = v - rac{\omega \cdot L}{2}$$ $$v_R = v + rac{\omega \cdot L}{2}\]

docs.title: Speed Ramping (Linear Acceleration)
docs.text:
To prevent the high-center-of-gravity torso (1.2m height) from tipping during acceleration, target wheel speeds are ramped gradually using a simple linear acceleration profile:

  Velocity
     ▲
Max ─┼───────/──────────────\───────
     │      /                     │     /                   0  ─┴────/────────────────────\────► Time
        Acc Ramp             Dec Ramp
* Acceleration Limit: Maximum acceleration is capped at \(0.4 ext{ m/s}^2\). * Deceleration Limit: Maximum deceleration is capped at \(0.6 ext{ m/s}^2\) during normal operations, and \(1.5 ext{ m/s}^2\) during emergency stops.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details how PRAYAS V1 is operated remotely over WAN/Internet connections, explaining the routing of latency-sensitive control commands.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
To control the robot from a remote location, commands are routed through a cloud-hosted VPS running an MQTT broker:

  [ Operator Web Client ] ───(MQTT over Secure Websockets)───> [ VPS Broker ]
                                                                      │
                                                            (MQTT / Wi-Fi)
                                                                      ▼
                                                              [ Master Node ]
  • Keep-Alive Ping: The remote operator's client sends a keep-alive message every 100 ms. If the Master Node does not receive this ping for more than 400 ms, it assumes the connection is lost and halts the robot.
  • Command Latency: Commands typically have a round-trip latency of 60–150 ms depending on the network, which is sufficient for safe indoor navigation.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the servo trajectory engine, joint limits, calibration settings, and coordinate mappings.

docs.title: Trajectory Interpolation (S-Curve)
docs.text:
To prevent jerky movements and reduce load on the structural frames, joint angles are interpolated using an S-curve profile:

\[ heta(t) = heta_{start} + ( heta_{end} - heta_{start}) \cdot \left( 3 \left( rac{t}{T} ight)^2 - 2 \left( rac{t}{T} ight)^3 ight)\]

This profile ensures smooth transitions by keeping joint acceleration at zero at the start and end of each movement.

docs.title: Coordinate Mapping
docs.text:
• Sample Range: The PCA9685 driver operates at a PWM frequency of 50 Hz (\(20 ext{ ms}\) period).
  • Resolution: 12-bit resolution (\(0\) to \(4095\) counts).
  • MG995 Pulse Range: The MG995 servo expects a pulse width between \(1.0 ext{ ms}\) (minimum angle, \(0^\circ\)) and \(2.0 ext{ ms}\) (maximum angle, \(180^\circ\)).
    • \(1.0 ext{ ms}\) pulse width corresponds to: $$ ext{Count}_{min} = rac{1.0 ext{ ms}}{20 ext{ ms}} imes 4096 pprox 205$$
    • \(2.0 ext{ ms}\) pulse width corresponds to: $$ ext{Count}_{max} = rac{2.0 ext{ ms}}{20 ext{ ms}} imes 4096 pprox 410$$

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document describes the mapping of voice commands to physical movements and gestures in PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: Voice Commands Map
docs.text:
Voice inputs processed by the AI Node (ESP32-S3) are categorized into intents and parameters, which are then routed to the Master Node over UART/ESP-NOW:

  • Command: "Move forward half a meter."
    • Intent: MOVE_DISTANCE
    • Target Subsystem: Motor Node
    • Action Parameters: { distance: 0.5, speed: 0.2 }
  • Command: "Turn right 90 degrees."
    • Intent: ROTATE_ANGLE
    • Target Subsystem: Motor Node
    • Action Parameters: { angle: 90.0, speed: 0.3 }
  • Command: "Stop immediately!"
    • Intent: ESTOP
    • Target Subsystem: Motor & Servo Nodes
    • Action Parameters: { instant: true }

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the configuration and features of the local Web Control Panel, which allows direct control of the robot over a local area network (LAN).

docs.title: Web Dashboard Features
docs.text: When the Master Node is in Local Access Point Mode (or connected to local Wi-Fi), it serves a lightweight web interface: * Virtual Joysticks: Two virtual joysticks (using nipplejs on the frontend) allow operators to control base movement and head positioning using a mobile phone or PC. * Telemetry Feeds: Displays real-time battery voltage, current draw, and E18-D80NK IR proximity sensor triggering states (Left, Center, Right). * Video Feed: Displays a live JPEG stream from the ESP32-CAM on port 81.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the kinematic configurations for the 3-DOF robotic arms, providing equations for joint positioning.

docs.title: DH Parameters & Joint Coordinates
docs.text:
Each arm has 3 Degrees of Freedom (Shoulder Pitch, Elbow Pitch, Wrist Roll). We calculate forward kinematics using the link lengths: * Link 1 (Upper Arm, Shoulder to Elbow): \(a_1 = 250 ext{ mm}\) * Link 2 (Forearm, Elbow to Wrist): \(a_2 = 220 ext{ mm}\)

       (Shoulder Joint: q1)
               o
              / \ 
             /   \ 
            /     \ Link 1 (a1 = 250mm)
           /       \ 
          /         \ 
         /           o (Elbow Joint: q2)
        /             \ 
                       \ Link 2 (a2 = 220mm)
                        \ 
                         o (Wrist Joint: q3)

docs.title: Kinematic Equations
docs.text:
Assuming planar movement for simplicity during forward calculations, the coordinates of the wrist (\(x, y\)) relative to the shoulder joint are:

\[x = a_1 \cos( heta_1) + a_2 \cos( heta_1 + heta_2)$$ $$y = a_1 \sin( heta_1) + a_2 \sin( heta_1 + heta_2)\]

Where $ heta_1$ and $ heta_2$ represent the shoulder and elbow joint angles.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document provides a step-by-step assembly guide for the PRAYAS V1 robot frame.

docs.title: Required Fasteners
docs.text:
• M5 Bolts (30mm): For securing the PVC floor flange to the plywood base.
  • M4 Machine Screws (15mm): For attaching motor brackets to the plywood deck.
  • M3 Bolts & Brass Inserts: For assembling 3D-printed joints and servo brackets.

docs.title: Step 1: Base Deck Assembly
docs.text:
1. Align the 4 Johnson motor steel brackets on the underside of the 12mm plywood base plate.
  2. Secure the brackets using M4 screws and washers.
  3. Mount the 4 geared motors into the brackets and tighten the set screws.
  4. Mount the customized metal flange hubs onto the motor shafts, align the 10cm diameter, 4cm width wheels, and bolt them securely to the hubs.
  5. Attach the 3 × E18-D80NK IR proximity sensors to their mounting brackets at the front-left, front-center, and front-right edges of the plywood base plate, and adjust the detection range potentiometer on each sensor.

docs.title: Step 2: Torso Spine Installation
docs.text:
1. Center the 4-inch PVC floor flange on the top side of the plywood base plate.
  2. Drill 4 holes through the plywood using the flange as a template.
  3. Secure the flange to the base deck using M5 bolts, washers, and locknuts.
  4. Apply PVC cement to the end of the 70 cm PVC pipe and insert it into the flange.

docs.title: Step 3: Torso and Limb Integration
docs.text:
1. Route the power and signal cables through the inside of the PVC pipe column.
  2. Mount the 3D-printed shoulder adapter onto the top of the PVC pipe and secure it with two through-bolts.
  3. Assemble the 3D-printed arm segments, inserting the MG995 servos into their brackets.
  4. Attach the arms to the shoulder frame.
  5. Install the neck servo and mount the head assembly on top of the shoulder frame.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document presents calculations for the Center of Gravity (COG) of PRAYAS V1, verifying the robot's physical stability.

docs.title: COG Estimation Method
docs.text:
We calculate the height of the COG (\(Z_{cog}\)) using the heights (\(Z_i\)) and masses (\(m_i\)) of the individual components, relative to the ground (\(Z = 0\)):

\[Z_{cog} = rac{\sum (m_i imes Z_i)}{\sum m_i}\]

docs.title: Component Heights and Mass
docs.text:
• Base & Wheels: \(m_1 = 3.3 ext{ kg}\), \(Z_1 = 80 ext{ mm}\)
  • Torso Column (PVC): \(m_2 = 1.0 ext{ kg}\), \(Z_2 = 510 ext{ mm}\) (midpoint of column)
  • Upper Torso & Servos: \(m_3 = 1.7 ext{ kg}\), \(Z_3 = 960 ext{ mm}\)
  • Battery System: \(m_4 = 0.5 ext{ kg}\), \(Z_4 = 100 ext{ mm}\)
  • Electronics & Wiring: \(m_5 = 0.5 ext{ kg}\), \(Z_5 = 450 ext{ mm}\)

docs.title: Stability Evaluation
docs.text:
The calculated COG height is approximately \(383 ext{ mm}\) (\(38.3 ext{ cm}\)) from the ground. Since the base footprint is \(400 ext{ mm} imes 350 ext{ mm}\) and the COG is low, the robot is highly stable. The tipover angle (\(lpha\)) is calculated as:

$\(lpha = rctan\left( rac{W_{base} / 2}{Z_{cog}} ight) = rctan\left( rac{175 ext{ mm}}{383 ext{ mm}} ight) pprox 24.5^\circ\)$ The robot can tilt up to \(24.5^\circ\) before tipping, ensuring safe operation on ramps and uneven indoor floors.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the mechanical design, motor mounts, and dynamics of the motorized base.

docs.title: Chassis Components
docs.text:
• Base Deck Plate: 12mm birch plywood deck.
  • Motors: 4 × Johnson 12V 200RPM geared motors.
  • Motor & Hub Mounting Brackets: 4 L-shaped heavy steel brackets bolted to the underside of the plywood deck using M4 machine screws, and 4 customized 100mm metal wheel hubs to mount the wheels to the motor shafts.
  • Wheels: 4 × Robot Wheels (10cm Diameter and 4cm Width) with high-traction rubber tread. The two left wheels and the two right wheels are coupled, providing a stable 4x4 differential drive base.
  • Obstacle Sensors: 3 × E18-D80NK Adjustable IR Proximity Sensors mounted on brackets at the front-left, front-center, and front-right edges of the base plate.

docs.title: Turning Mechanics
docs.text:
The motorized base uses a differential drive configuration to turn: 

     Left Motors Forward (▲)       Left Motors Reverse (▼)
     Right Motors Reverse (▼)      Right Motors Forward (▲)
          ┌─────────┐                   ┌─────────┐
          │   ▲   ▲ │                   │   ▼   ▲ │
          │         │                   │         │
          │   ▼   ▼ │                   │   ▲   ▼ │
          └─────────┘                   └─────────┘
         Spin Right                     Spin Left

  • Total Height: \(1150 ext{ mm}\) (approx. \(1.15\) meters).
  • Base Plate footprint: \(400 ext{ mm} imes 350 ext{ mm}\) rectangular deck.
  • Base Height (including wheels): \(160 ext{ mm}\) off the ground.
  • Spine Column (PVC): \(700 ext{ mm}\) height, \(4.5 ext{ in}\) outer diameter (\(114.3 ext{ mm}\)).
  • Shoulder-to-Shoulder Span: \(320 ext{ mm}\).
  • Arm Length: \(470 ext{ mm}\) (Shoulder-to-Elbow: \(250 ext{ mm}\), Elbow-to-Wrist: \(220 ext{ mm}\)).
  • Ground Clearance: \(30 ext{ mm}\) under the lowest base bracket.

docs.title: Alignment and Offsets
docs.text: During assembly, the servos must be set to their mid-point positions (\(90^\circ\)) before attaching the horns to align the limbs with the robot's zero-position references.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the weight distribution across the PRAYAS V1 subassemblies, ensuring balance and structural stability.

docs.title: Mass Budgets
docs.text:
The estimated masses of the robot's components are as follows:

Subassembly Material / Components Estimated Mass (kg) Percentage of Total Lower Base 12mm Plywood, steel brackets, shroud 1.8 kg 25.7% Drivetrain 4 Johnson motors, 4 rubber wheels 1.5 kg 21.4% Torso Spine 4-inch PVC pipe (70cm length) 1.0 kg 14.3% Upper Torso 3D Printed panels and electronics 1.2 kg 17.1% Limb Servos 7 × MG995 servos and mounts 0.5 kg 7.1% Battery 3S2P Lithium cell pack 0.5 kg 7.1% Electronics Wiring, regulators, sensors 0.5 kg 7.1% Total 7.0 kg 100%

docs.title: Design Recommendations
docs.text: To ensure stability, the heaviest components (the battery and motor drivers) are mounted directly to the plywood base plate. This design keeps the center of gravity low and prevents tipping during acceleration.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the types of connectors and terminals used in PRAYAS V1 to ensure secure electrical connections.

docs.title: Crimping Standards
docs.text: • All JST and Dupont connections must be crimped using a dedicated tool (e.g. SN-28B) and protected with heat-shrink tubing to prevent short circuits.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document presents the current budget calculations and estimated runtime for PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: Current Budgets
docs.text:
Below is the current budget under nominal and peak load conditions:

Component Quantity Idle Current (A) Max / Stall Current (A) Operational Rail ESP32 Node (Logic) 5 0.40 1.20 5.0 V ESP32-CAM 1 0.15 0.40 5.0 V Arduino Nano 33 1 0.05 0.10 5.0 V MG995 Servo 7 0.70 (0.1A each) 10.50 (1.5A stall each) 6.0 V Johnson DC Motor 4 1.60 (0.4A each) 8.00 (2.0A stall each) 12.0 V Total System 2.90 A 20.20 A

docs.title: Operational Runtime Calculations
docs.text:
Calculations are based on a 3S 6800 mAh Li-ion battery (\(75.48 ext{ Wh}\)):

  • Idle Mode (Only Microcontrollers and Sensors active): $\(P_{idle} pprox 0.6 ext{ A} imes 5 ext{V} pprox 3.0 ext{ W} \implies ext{Runtime} pprox rac{75.48 ext{ Wh}}{3.0 ext{ W}} pprox 25 ext{ Hours}\)$
  • Mixed Interaction Mode (Typical movements, voice, and driving): $\(P_{mixed} pprox 28.0 ext{ W} \implies ext{Runtime} pprox rac{75.48 ext{ Wh}}{28.0 ext{ W}} pprox 2.7 ext{ Hours}\)$
  • Heavy Workload Mode (Continuous driving and joint movements): $\(P_{max} pprox 65.0 ext{ W} \implies ext{Runtime} pprox rac{75.48 ext{ Wh}}{65.0 ext{ W}} pprox 1.1 ext{ Hours}\)$

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document outlines the safety fuses used in PRAYAS V1 to protect the battery and regulators from overcurrent damage.

docs.title: Fuse Specifications
docs.text:
• Main Battery Fuse: A 15A blade fuse (standard automotive ATC size) is installed inline on the battery's positive cable (\(14 ext{ AWG}\)). This fuse prevents fire hazards in the event of a direct short on the main power bus.
  • Servo Regulator Fuse: A 10A fuse is installed on the input of the 6V regulator to protect the regulator from servo overloads.
  • Logic Regulator Fuse: A 3A inline fuse is installed on the input of the 5V regulator.

docs.title: Fuse Location Diagram
docs.text:
[ Battery (+) Terminal ] ─── [ 15A Inline Fuse ] ─── [ Main Power Switch ]
                                                              │
                                            ┌─────────────────┴─────────────────┐
                                            ▼                                   ▼
                                   [ 10A Inline Fuse ]                 [ 3A Inline Fuse ]
                                            │                                   │
                                 [ 6V Servo Regulator ]              [ 5V Logic Regulator ]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document provides a single-point-of-truth GPIO mapping table for all microcontroller nodes in PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: 1. Master Coordinator Node (ESP32)
docs.text:
• GPIO 16: UART Rx2 (Connected to AI Node Tx)
  • GPIO 17: UART Tx2 (Connected to AI Node Rx)
  • GPIO 21: I2C SDA (OLED Display / Status Display)
  • GPIO 22: I2C SCL (OLED Display / Status Display)

docs.title: 2. Motor Controller Node (ESP32)
docs.text:

  • GPIO 13: Left Motor PWM Reverse (R_PWM)

  • GPIO 25: Right Motor PWM Forward (L_PWM)
  • GPIO 26: Right Motor PWM Reverse (R_PWM)
  • GPIO 27: Right Driver Enable (L_EN / R_EN)
  • GPIO 34: Left IR Proximity Sensor Input (E18-D80NK, Active LOW)
  • GPIO 35: Center IR Proximity Sensor Input (E18-D80NK, Active LOW)

  • On-board Camera Bus: Connected to integrated camera module slot via internal GPIOs
  • GPIO 2 (Pin D8): I2S Serial Data Out (To ES8311 DAC SDIN)
  • GPIO 8 (Pin D7): I2S Serial Clock (To ES8311 BCLK)
  • GPIO 9 (Pin D9): I2S Word Select / LRCK (To ES8311 WS)
  • GPIO 43 (Pin D1): UART Tx (Connected to Master Node GPIO 16)
  • GPIO 44 (Pin D2): UART Rx (Connected to Master Node GPIO 17)

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the configuration and specifications of the three isolated power rails in PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: Voltage Rail Specifications Table
docs.text: Rail Name Target Voltage Peak Current Wire Gauge Regulating Device Connected Loads Motor Rail 12.0V 10.0 A 14 AWG Direct battery output Dual BTS7960 H-bridges Servo Rail 6.0V 12.0 A 16 AWG High-Current Step-Down Buck 7 × MG995 servos via PCA9685 Logic Rail 5.0V 5.0 A 20 AWG LM2596 Step-Down Buck ESP32s, ESP32-S3, ESP32-CAM Sensors Rail 3.3V 1.0 A 24 AWG Onboard ESP32 regulators (LDO) Nano 33 BLE, digital sensors

docs.title: Isolated Regulator Routing
docs.text: Separate buck regulators are used for the logic and servo rails. This prevents voltage drops caused by the high current draw of the servos from resetting the microcontrollers.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the protection circuits used in PRAYAS V1 to guard against reverse polarity, voltage spikes, and electromagnetic interference.

docs.title: 1. Reverse Polarity Protection
docs.text: A high-current Schottky diode (or a P-channel MOSFET circuit) is connected in series with the input of the buck regulators. This prevents damage to the electronics if the battery is connected backward.

docs.title: 2. Back-EMF Snubbers
docs.text: When the Johnson DC motors stop, they generate back-EMF voltage spikes. The BTS7960 drivers include internal flyback diodes to dissipate these spikes. Additionally, a \(0.1\mu ext{F}\) ceramic capacitor is soldered across each motor's terminal pins to suppress high-frequency noise.

docs.title: 3. TVS Diodes
docs.text: Transient Voltage Suppressor (TVS) diodes (e.g. 5KP15A) are installed across the input of the 5V logic regulator. These diodes clamp transient voltage spikes to a safe level, protecting the microcontrollers.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document outlines the voltage levels and the characteristics of the regulators used in PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: 1. 6.0V Servo Regulator (SZBK07)
docs.text:
• Type: Synchronous Step-Down Buck Converter.
  • Input Voltage Range: 9V to 40V.
  • Output Voltage: 6.0V (adjusted via onboard potentiometer).
  • Maximum Continuous Current: 12A (15A peak with forced air cooling).
  • Efficiency: ~94%.

docs.title: 2. 5.0V Logic Regulator (LM2596)
docs.text:
• Type: Step-Down Switching Regulator.
  • Input Voltage Range: 7V to 35V.
  • Output Voltage: 5.0V (fixed or adjusted).
  • Maximum Continuous Current: 3A.
  • Efficiency: ~80%.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document describes the routing and wiring connections between the nodes, sensors, and actuators of PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: Cable Routing Guidelines
docs.text:
• Power Lines: Wire gauges for the battery connections and motor driver inputs must be at least 14 AWG to prevent voltage drops.
  • Signal Lines: Keep I2C and I2S signal lines shorter than 15 cm and route them away from power cables to prevent electromagnetic interference (EMI).

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the implementation and packet format of the ESP-NOW communication protocol used for low-latency inter-node communication.

docs.title: Protocol Characteristics
docs.text:
• Type: Connectionless, peer-to-peer 802.11 physical layer wireless packets.
  • Frequency Band: Fixed to Wi-Fi Channel 6 (\(2437 ext{ MHz}\)).
  • Target Latency: < 5 ms.
  • Max Payload: 250 bytes per packet.

docs.title: Shared Data Structures
docs.text:
All nodes share a common data structure defined in a shared header file esp_now_protocol.h:

typedef enum {
    MSG_TYPE_HEARTBEAT,
    MSG_TYPE_MOTOR_CMD,
    MSG_TYPE_SERVO_CMD,
    MSG_TYPE_SENSOR_DATA,
    MSG_TYPE_ESTOP
} MessageType;

typedef struct {
    uint8_t message_type;
    uint32_t timestamp;
    uint8_t payload[240];
} ESPNowPacket;

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the heartbeat watchdogs used to detect communication failures and automatically trigger safety halts.

docs.title: Heartbeat Mechanism
docs.text:
The Master Node broadcasts a heartbeat packet to all sub-nodes every 200 ms:

  Master Node  ───(ESP-NOW: MSG_TYPE_HEARTBEAT)───>  Sub-Nodes (Motor, Servo)
  • Heartbeat Frequency: 200 ms.
  • Timeout Threshold: 500 ms.
  • Fail-Safe Action: If a sub-node does not receive a heartbeat packet within 500 ms, it immediately enters a Safe Halt State (motors stopped, servos held in current positions).

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document provides JSON schemas for the MQTT messages used to communicate with the Web Dashboard.

docs.title: 1. Telemetry Status Schema (prayas/telemetry/state)
docs.text:
{
  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
  "title": "PrayasTelemetryState",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "timestamp": { "type": "integer" },
    "battery": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "voltage": { "type": "number", "minimum": 0.0 },
        "current": { "type": "number" },
        "percentage": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 100 }
      },
      "required": ["voltage", "current", "percentage"]
    },
    "sensors": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "proximity_left": { "type": "boolean" },
        "proximity_center": { "type": "boolean" },
        "proximity_right": { "type": "boolean" },
        "temperature": { "type": "number" }
      },
      "required": ["proximity_left", "proximity_center", "proximity_right"]
    },
    "system_state": { "type": "string" }
  },
  "required": ["timestamp", "battery", "sensors", "system_state"]
}

docs.title: 2. Base Command Move Schema (prayas/command/move)
docs.text:
{
  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
  "title": "PrayasCommandMove",
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "linear_x": { "type": "number", "minimum": -0.8, "maximum": 0.8 },
    "angular_z": { "type": "number", "minimum": -1.5, "maximum": 1.5 }
  },
  "required": ["linear_x", "angular_z"]
}

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the configuration and topics used for MQTT communication between the Master Node and the cloud broker.

docs.title: Broker Settings
docs.text:
• Broker Host: Deployed on a Virtual Private Server (VPS) at mqtt.prayas-robotics.org.
  • Port: 1883 (Standard TCP), 8883 (Secure TLS/SSL).
  • Quality of Service (QoS):
    • QoS 0 (At most once): Used for high-frequency telemetry updates.
    • QoS 1 (At least once): Used for command messages and configuration updates.

docs.title: Topic Mapping Table
docs.text: MQTT Topic Direction Payload Type Description prayas/telemetry/state Publish JSON Publishes battery levels, sensor statuses, and node states at 5 Hz. prayas/command/move Subscribe JSON Receives linear and angular velocity commands for the base. prayas/command/gesture Subscribe JSON Receives gesture command IDs (e.g. Wave, Point, Bow). prayas/command/estop Subscribe JSON Receives immediate emergency stop commands.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document defines the packet serialization and deserialization formats used for communication between the nodes.

docs.title: Communication Modes
docs.text:
1. High-Frequency Control Loop (ESP-NOW): Used for time-critical commands (such as motor velocities and servo angles). Packets are serialized as raw binary structs to minimize processing overhead.
  2. Telemetry and Web Communication (JSON): Used for configuration files, debug logs, and dashboard telemetry. Packets are formatted as JSON text strings to simplify integration with web technologies.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the transmission retry logic used to ensure reliable delivery of critical commands.

docs.title: ESP-NOW Transmission Verification
docs.text:
Unlike standard Wi-Fi, ESP-NOW does not establish a persistent connection. To ensure critical packets (like gesture triggers or state changes) are delivered, the Master Node uses a confirm-and-retry mechanism:

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SEND_PACKET
    SEND_PACKET --> WAIT_ACK : Packet Sent

    WAIT_ACK --> SUCCESS : ACK Received

    WAIT_ACK --> RETRY_PACKET : Timeout (10 ms) & Retry < 3
    RETRY_PACKET --> WAIT_ACK

    WAIT_ACK --> DISCONNECT_FAULT : Timeout (10 ms) & Retry >= 3
    DISCONNECT_FAULT --> [*]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document describes the state synchronization mechanism, which ensures all nodes have a consistent view of the robot's current state.

docs.title: State Synchronization Flow
docs.text:
To prevent inconsistent states (such as the robot trying to drive while in a calibration mode), the Master Node maintains the master state and periodically broadcasts it to all sub-nodes:

sequenceDiagram
    participant Master as Master Node
    participant Motor as Motor Node
    participant Servo as Servo Node

    Master->>Motor: State Broadcast: STATE_IDLE
    Master->>Servo: State Broadcast: STATE_IDLE
    Note over Master, Motor: User triggers move command
    Master->>Motor: Send Speed Command
    Master->>Motor: State Broadcast: STATE_MOVING
    Master->>Servo: State Broadcast: STATE_MOVING

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the manual controls panel, which allows users to drive the base and trigger gestures.

docs.title: Control Interfaces
docs.text:
• Virtual Joysticks: NippleJS virtual joysticks allow driving the motorized base and panning the camera.
  • Quick Gestures: Buttons to trigger preconfigured servo animations (e.g. wave, point, look-around).
  • Emergency Stop: A prominent red button that immediately halts all motors and joints.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the layout, styling, and framework integration of the PRAYAS Web Dashboard.

docs.title: UI Architecture
docs.text:
The PRAYAS Web Dashboard is built as a single-page web application using core HTML5, CSS3 (Vanilla), and JavaScript. It provides a visual interface for managing telemetry, camera feeds, logs, and manual controls.

graph TD
    Client[Web Dashboard Client] -->|HTTP / port 80| Master[Master Node Webserver]
    Client -->|WebSockets / port 81| Camera[Camera Node Video Stream]
    Client -->|MQTT over WebSockets / port 9001| Broker[VPS MQTT Broker]
    Broker <-->|MQTT / port 1883| Master

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the dashboard log viewer, which aggregates diagnostics messages from all nodes.

docs.title: Log Terminal Features
docs.text: The log viewer subscribes to the MQTT logging topic prayas/log/output and displays messages in a scrollable console window. It allows filtering logs by node name (e.g. Master, Motor) or severity level (e.g. Warn, Error).

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the diagnostics screen, which shows the status and connection health of all nodes.

docs.title: Diagnostic Checks
docs.text: The diagnostics panel queries the status of the nodes: * Node Handshake Status: Green/Red indicator showing node connection state. * Memory Health: Displays free heap and stack allocations. * Packet Count: Logs the number of sent/received ESP-NOW packets and tracks transmission errors.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the Web OTA update interface, which allows developers to update firmware on the nodes wirelessly.

docs.title: OTA Workflow
docs.text: Through the dashboard, developers can compile binaries and flash nodes wirelessly: 1. Node Selection: Select the target node (Master, Motor, Servo, or AI Node). 2. Binary Selection: Drag-and-drop the compiled .bin file. 3. Flash Request: Trigger the update. The dashboard sends an HTTP POST request to the target node's IP, which writes the binary to the inactive flash partition and reboots.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the telemetry panel, explaining how sensor and battery data are visualized.

docs.title: Display Elements
docs.text: The telemetry panel updates at 5 Hz and displays: * Battery Gauge: High-contrast progress bar showing voltage, current draw, and charge percentage (\(0 ext{ to }100\%\)). * System Temperature: Visual thermometer displaying internal electronics bay temperature. * Sonar Distance Maps: High-contrast radar charts showing distances to obstacles.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the video panel, which displays the live camera feed from the robot.

docs.title: MJPEG Streaming
docs.text:
The dashboard streams video from the ESP32-CAM over WebSockets on port 81, bypassing the MQTT broker to minimize latency: 

  [ ESP32-CAM Node ] ──(WS: JPEG Frames)──> [ HTML Canvas Element ] ──> [ Live Display ]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the test plan to verify battery capacity, discharge curves, and BMS safety cut-offs.

docs.title: Test Procedure
docs.text:
1. Capacity Test: Discharge the battery pack from \(12.6 ext{V}\) to \(9.0 ext{V}\) under a constant 3.4A load, verifying that capacity meets the 6800 mAh specification.
  2. BMS Cut-off Verification: Under a controlled discharge, verify that the BMS cuts power when any cell drops below \(2.8 ext{V}\).

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the test plan to verify inter-node communication latency and packet loss.

docs.title: Test Procedure
docs.text:
1. ESP-NOW Ping Test: Run a diagnostics script that sends a ping packet from the Master Node to the Motor and Servo Nodes, logging round-trip latency.
  2. Timeout Verification: Disconnect the Master Node and verify that all sub-nodes enter their safe states within 500 ms.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the test plan to verify motor direction, driver speed control, and encoder outputs.

docs.title: Test Procedure
docs.text:
1. H-Bridge Direction Test: Suspend the robot base. Run a script that drives each wheel forward and backward at 20% duty cycle, verifying wheel direction.
  2. Current Load Test: Drive the base on flat ground. Monitor current draw to ensure it remains below 3.0A under normal conditions, and does not exceed the 15A fuse rating under stall conditions.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the test plan to verify Wi-Fi and Bluetooth signal strength, range, and latency.

docs.title: Test Procedure
docs.text:
1. RSSI Mapping: Move the robot away from the Wi-Fi AP. Log the Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) value at 5-meter increments up to 30 meters.
  2. Packet Delivery Rate: Verify that the packet drop rate remains below 1% under normal operating conditions.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the electrical test plan to verify voltage levels, ripple currents, and isolation between rails.

docs.title: Test Procedure
docs.text: Before connecting any microcontroller nodes, verify the output voltage of the buck converters under load using a multimeter: * Servo Rail: Verify voltage is \(6.0 ext{V} \pm 0.1 ext{V}\) under a 5A load. * Logic Rail: Verify voltage is \(5.0 ext{V} \pm 0.1 ext{V}\) under a 2A load.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document outlines the test plan to verify servo range-of-motion, calibration offsets, and current draw.

docs.title: Test Procedure
docs.text:
1. Logic Power-up: Apply 5V logic power to the Servo Node (with the 6V servo rail disconnected) and verify PCA9685 I2C communications.
  2. Actuator Verification: Apply 6V power. Command each servo to move to its home position (\(90^\circ\)) and verify alignment.
  3. Sweep Testing: Run a sweep test from \(15^\circ\) to \(165^\circ\) at \(10^\circ/ ext{sec}\), monitoring current draw to ensure it does not exceed 1.0A per servo.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the stress testing procedure, which evaluates system stability under heavy load conditions.

docs.title: Test Procedure
docs.text: Run the robot continuously for 45 minutes under maximum load: * Drive the motorized base in a figure-eight pattern. * Run continuous arm gesture loops and neck panning motions. * Stream live video from the ESP32-CAM and run the AI Node conversation loop. Verify that regulator temperatures remain below \(75^\circ ext{C}\) and that no microcontroller resets occur.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the slicing settings and printing parameters for the robot's 3D-printed structural components.

docs.title: Printing Configurations Table
docs.text: Parameter Structural Parts (PETG) Cosmetic Covers (PLA+) Nozzle Temperature \(240^\circ   ext{C}\) to \(245^\circ   ext{C}\) \(210^\circ    ext{C}\) to \(215^\circ   ext{C}\) Bed Temperature \(80^\circ   ext{C}\) \(60^\circ ext{C}\) Infill Density 40% (Gyroid) 15% (Grid) Perimeters (Walls) 4 2 Layer Height 0.20 mm 0.20 mm Cooling Fan 30% (for strength) 100% (for detail)

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the recommended assembly sequence for the PRAYAS V1 robot frame.

docs.title: Assembly Steps
docs.text:
1. Base Assembly: Mount the motors and drivers to the plywood deck. Install the wheels.
  2. Torso Column Installation: Bolt the PVC flange to the base, insert the PVC column, and secure the wiring harness inside the pipe.
  3. Upper Torso Installation: Mount the shoulder adapter, upper torso panels, and electronics boards.
  4. Arm Assembly: Mount the servos into the arm brackets and attach the arm segments to the shoulder joints.
  5. Head Integration: Mount the neck servo and head shell. Route cables to the electronics bay.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the calibration procedures for the sensors and servos of PRAYAS V1.

docs.title: Calibration Steps
docs.text:
1. Servo Neutral Alignment: Set all servos to their neutral position (\(90^\circ\), 1.5ms pulse width) and align the limbs.
  2. IMU Calibration: Place the robot on a flat, level surface and run the calibration script to calculate accelerometer and gyroscope offsets.
  3. E18-D80NK Proximity Sensor Range Adjustment: Use a small screwdriver to adjust the multi-turn potentiometer on the back of each of the 3 E18-D80NK IR sensors. Turn clockwise to increase range (up to 80cm) or counterclockwise to decrease it. Calibrate each sensor so that it pulls its output LOW (triggered) when a barrier is within 20cm.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details potential manufacturing upgrades to improve the durability and appearance of future robot versions.

docs.title: Proposed Upgrades
docs.text:
• Injection-molded or vacuum-formed casing: Replace the sunboard covers with vacuum-formed ABS plastic shells to improve durability and appearance.
  • Aluminum Torso Column: Replace the PVC pipe column with an anodized aluminum tube to increase rigidity.
  • Serial Bus Servos: Upgrade the analog MG995 servos to serial bus smart servos (e.g. Lewansoul/Feetech STS series) to allow position and temperature feedback.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document outlines the maintenance tasks and intervals to ensure the long-term reliability of the robot.

docs.title: Maintenance Tasks
docs.text:
• Every 10 Operating Hours: Inspect motor mounts and frame fasteners, tightening any loose screws.
  • Every 50 Operating Hours: Inspect the wheels for wear. Check joint play and grease the servo gears if necessary.
  • Every 100 Operating Hours: Check the battery pack for signs of swelling. Clean dust from the electronics bay and ensure fans are running smoothly.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the preparation, cutting, and drilling instructions for raw materials like plywood and PVC.

docs.title: Fabrication Steps
docs.text:
1. Plywood Base Plate: Cut a \(400 ext{ mm} imes 350 ext{ mm}\) rectangle from a sheet of 12mm birch plywood. Drill holes for motor mounts, routing brackets, and the PVC floor flange.
  2. Torso Spine: Cut a 70cm length of 4-inch Schedule 40 PVC pipe. Drill 20mm routing holes near the top and bottom of the pipe to allow routing signal and power cables.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document lists the custom parts that must be fabricated to assemble the PRAYAS V1 robot.

docs.title: Custom Fabricated Parts
docs.text:
• Plywood Base Deck: Rectangular base plate.
  • Torso Spine PVC Pipe: Structural column.
  • 3D Printed Shoulder Adapter: Mounts the upper torso to the PVC pipe.
  • 3D Printed Upper Torso Panels: Houses the head servo, electronics, and arm mounts.
  • 3D Printed Arm Segments: Custom brackets for the MG995 servos.
  • Sunboard Base Cover: Shroud panels that attach to the plywood deck to cover the motors and battery.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the MQTT topic structure and JSON schemas used to communicate with the cloud broker and Web Dashboard.

docs.title: 1. Base Locomotion Command
docs.text:
• Topic: prayas/command/move
  • Access: Write-Only
  • Payload Format: JSON
  • Payload Example: 
    {
  "linear_x": 0.5,
  "angular_z": -0.2
}

docs.title: 2. Servo Gesture Trigger
docs.text:
• Topic: prayas/command/gesture
  • Access: Write-Only
  • Payload Format: JSON
  • Payload Example: 
    {
  "gesture_id": 1,
  "duration": 2500
}

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the primary C++ functions used in the Motor Node firmware to control the base motors.

docs.title: 1. void setBaseSpeed(float linearX, float angularZ)
docs.text:
• Description: Updates target wheel speeds and applies the acceleration ramp.
  • Parameters:
    • linearX: Target linear velocity (m/s).
    • angularZ: Target rotation rate (rad/s).

docs.title: 2. void triggerEmergencyStop()
docs.text:
• Description: Instantly disables motor outputs and engages the brakes.
  • Implementation: 
    void triggerEmergencyStop() {
    // Force H-bridge enable lines to LOW
    digitalWrite(LEFT_EN_PIN, LOW);
    digitalWrite(RIGHT_EN_PIN, LOW);
    // Force all H-bridge PWM inputs to LOW
    ledcWrite(LEFT_PWM_F_CHAN, 0);
    ledcWrite(LEFT_PWM_R_CHAN, 0);
    ledcWrite(RIGHT_PWM_F_CHAN, 0);
    ledcWrite(RIGHT_PWM_R_CHAN, 0);
}

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document provides a programming reference for the ESP-NOW communication protocol used between the PRAYAS V1 microcontroller nodes.

docs.title: Data Structures
docs.text:
All nodes send and receive messages using a unified packet structure defined in esp_now_protocol.h:

typedef enum {
    MSG_TYPE_HEARTBEAT     = 0x01,
    MSG_TYPE_MOTOR_CMD     = 0x02,
    MSG_TYPE_SERVO_CMD     = 0x03,
    MSG_TYPE_SENSOR_DATA   = 0x04,
    MSG_TYPE_ESTOP         = 0xFF
} MessageType;

typedef struct __attribute__((packed)) {
    uint8_t msg_type;        // 1 byte: MessageType enum
    uint32_t timestamp;      // 4 bytes: Milliseconds since boot
    uint8_t payload[240];    // 240 bytes: Command or telemetry payload
} ESPNowPacket;

docs.title: 1. Motor Command Payload (MSG_TYPE_MOTOR_CMD)
docs.text:
Sent by the Master Node to the Motor Node to set linear and angular velocities: 

typedef struct __attribute__((packed)) {
    float linear_x;          // 4 bytes: Linear velocity in m/s (-0.8 to 0.8)
    float angular_z;         // 4 bytes: Angular velocity in rad/s (-1.5 to 1.5)
} MotorCommandPayload;

docs.title: 2. Servo Command Payload (MSG_TYPE_SERVO_CMD)
docs.text:
Sent by the Master Node to the Servo Node to set a target joint angle: 

typedef struct __attribute__((packed)) {
    uint8_t joint_id;        // 1 byte: Joint index (0 to 6)
    uint16_t target_angle;   // 2 bytes: Target angle in degrees (0 to 180)
    uint16_t duration_ms;    // 2 bytes: Trajectory interpolation duration
} ServoCommandPayload;

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the primary C++ functions used in the Servo Node firmware to configure and drive the servos.

docs.title: 1. void initServoDriver()
docs.text:
• Description: Initializes the I2C bus and sets the PCA9685 PWM frequency.
  • Implementation: 
    void initServoDriver() {
    Wire.begin(SDA_PIN, SCL_PIN, 400000); // 400 kHz Fast I2C
    pwmDriver.begin();
    pwmDriver.setOscillatorFrequency(27000000);
    pwmDriver.setPWMFreq(50);             // 50 Hz for analog servos
    pinMode(OE_PIN, OUTPUT);
    digitalWrite(OE_PIN, LOW);            // Enable outputs
}

docs.title: 2. void setJointAngle(uint8_t jointId, float angle)
docs.text:
• Description: Writes a target angle directly to the specified joint.
  • Parameters:
    • jointId: The index of the target servo.
    • angle: Target angle in degrees (\(0.0^\circ\) to \(180.0^\circ\)).

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details the telemetry packet structure used to transmit system status data.

docs.title: Telemetry Fields Schema
docs.text:
The telemetry data published to prayas/telemetry/state includes the following fields:

Field Name Type Unit Description timestamp Integer ms Time elapsed since Master Node boot. battery.voltage Float V Battery voltage read from the analog sensor (range \(9.0 ext{V}\) to \(12.6 ext{V}\)). battery.current Float A Current draw read from the ACS712 sensor. sensors.proximity_left Boolean - Status of Left E18-D80NK IR Proximity sensor (true if triggered/LOW). sensors.proximity_center Boolean - Status of Center E18-D80NK IR Proximity sensor (true if triggered/LOW). sensors.proximity_right Boolean - Status of Right E18-D80NK IR Proximity sensor (true if triggered/LOW). system_state String - Current state (e.g. STATE_IDLE, STATE_MOVING, STATE_FAULT).

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document establishes coding standards and conventions for the PRAYAS V1 firmware and software repositories.

docs.title: 1. C++ Guidelines
docs.text:
• Indentation: Use 4 spaces for indentation (no tabs).
  • Variable Names: Use camelCase for variables and parameters (e.g. targetVelocity, jointId).
  • Function Names: Use camelCase (e.g. setBaseSpeed(), readBatteryVoltage()).
  • Constants: Use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants and macro definitions (e.g. MAX_SPEED_MPS, WIFI_CHANNEL).
  • Classes: Use PascalCase for class and struct names (e.g. MotorController, GestureEngine).

docs.title: 2. Comments & Doxygen
docs.text:
All functions must include Doxygen-compatible comment blocks detailing the purpose, parameters, and return values: 

/**
 * @brief Computes wheel velocities for differential drive kinematics.
 * @param linearX Forward velocity command in m/s.
 * @param angularZ Rotational velocity command in rad/s.
 */
void computeKinematics(float linearX, float angularZ);

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document details how developers and open-source contributors can report issues, propose features, and submit code to the project.

docs.title: Contribution Workflow
docs.text:
1. Fork the Repository: Fork the target project repository on GitHub.
  2. Create a Feature Branch: Branch off develop using a descriptive name: 
    git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name develop
  3. Implement Changes: Write code adhering to the project's Coding Standards.
  4. Run Tests: Run unit tests and verify the code builds locally.
  5. Submit a Pull Request (PR): Target your PR to merge into the develop branch. Detail the changes made, the issue fixed, and the testing performed.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This document outlines the Git branching strategy and commit message guidelines for developers contributing to the project.

docs.title: Branching Strategy
docs.text: PRAYAS repositories use a simplified feature-branch workflow: * main: Holds the stable release code. Direct commits to main are blocked. * develop: The integration branch for current features. * feature/feature-name: Temporary branch used for developing new features. * bugfix/bug-name: Temporary branch used for fixing bugs.

docs.title: Commit Message Format
docs.text: Commit messages must follow the Conventional Commits specification: <type>(<scope>): <description>

docs.title: Standard Types
docs.text:
• feat: Adding a new feature.
  • fix: Fixing a bug.
  • docs: Documentation changes.
  • style: Code formatting changes (whitespace, semicolons).
  • refactor: Code changes that do not fix bugs or add features.
  • test: Adding or updating test code.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
• feat(motor): implement PID acceleration ramping
  • fix(servo): resolve I2C lockup during initialization
  • docs(wiring): update GPIO pin mapping table
